metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — first-time screening colonoscopy consult driven by strong family history of colorectal cancer

prior_notes.text: 65-year-old man presents for annual physical, overdue for colorectal cancer screening — has never undergone colonoscopy or stool-based testing. Notable strong family history of colon cancer: mother diagnosed in her late 40s, a brother diagnosed in his early 50s. Patient is asymptomatic — denies hematochezia, melena, change in bowel habits, or unintentional weight loss. No personal history of cancer, polyps, or IBD. Exam unremarkable. Takes no medications, no anticoagulants/antiplatelets. Referring to Gastroenterology for screening colonoscopy given family history; given ages of affected relatives, consider hereditary cancer evaluation.

Adult health maintenance. Well, no complaints. BP and metabolic panel within normal limits. Recurrent discussion that patient is due for colon cancer screening; patient had deferred previously. Reinforced importance given family history. Labs today notable for normal CBC (Hgb 14.8). No medications. Plan: encourage colonoscopy scheduling.

reasonCode.text: Screening indicated by family history of colorectal cancer
Two first-degree relatives with young-onset colorectal cancer

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Banks. Thanks for coming in. Your primary care doctor sent you over to get set up for a colonoscopy. Tell me what brings you in.
MA: Before the doctor started — let me get your vitals. Height's 5 foot 10, weight 185 pounds. Temp 36.8, heart rate 72, respirations 14, blood pressure 122 over 78, and your oxygen's 98 percent on room air. Great, all set.
PT: Well, honestly it's the family history that has everybody worried. My doctor's been on me for years to get this done and I kept putting it off. Figured it's finally time.
DR: I appreciate you coming in. Let me make sure I have the history right. The referral mentions colon cancer in the family — can you walk me through who?
PT: Yeah. My mother had colon cancer. She was young — forty-eight when they found it. And my brother, my older brother, he was diagnosed at fifty-two.
DR: I'm sorry to hear that. And to be clear — for you yourself, have you ever been told you had cancer, or had any polyps removed?
PT: No, no. Never me. I've never even had the test done, this'll be my first one. It's them — my mom and my brother.
DR: That's an important distinction, and thank you for being clear. So this is a screening for you, not because of anything you're feeling. Any symptoms at all — bleeding when you go, blood in the stool, change in your bowel habits, weight loss?
PT: None of that. No bleeding, nothing like that. I feel fine, really.
DR: Good. Any medications you take regularly? Any blood thinners, aspirin, anything like that?
PT: Nope. I don't take anything. No pills at all.
DR: Any allergies to medications?
PT: Not that I know of.
DR: Okay. Here's my thinking. Because your mother and brother were both diagnosed young — under fifty-five — that puts you in a higher-risk group, so we screen earlier and more often than average. I'd also like to talk with you about seeing a genetic counselor, because that pattern can sometimes run in families in a way worth testing for.
PT: Whatever you think is best. I just want to get ahead of it.
DR: That's the right instinct. We'll schedule the colonoscopy, send you the prep instructions, and since you're on no blood thinners there's nothing to stop beforehand. I'll put in the genetics referral for you to consider.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Banks is a 65-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for a first-time screening colonoscopy consultation, referred by primary care due to a strong family history of colorectal cancer. He reports his mother was diagnosed with colon cancer at age 48 and his brother at age 52. He has never undergone colorectal cancer screening. Importantly, the patient has no personal history of cancer or polyps — the malignancies are in his relatives, not himself. He is asymptomatic: denies rectal bleeding, hematochezia, melena, change in bowel habits, and unintentional weight loss. He takes no prescription medications and no antiplatelet/anticoagulant agents. No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/78, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 84 kg (185 lb), BMI 26.5. Recent labs (2026-06-25): hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL, hematocrit 45.0%, platelets 244 x10^3/uL — all within normal limits, no evidence of anemia or occult blood loss.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Average-risk symptoms but HIGH-RISK by family history: two first-degree relatives (mother age 48, brother age 52) with young-onset colorectal cancer. This drives an earlier and shortened screening interval; it is NOT a personal cancer diagnosis.
- Plan screening colonoscopy — indication is family history, patient asymptomatic.
- Hereditary cancer risk: young-onset CRC in two first-degree relatives raises concern for a Lynch-syndrome (HNPCC) pattern. Offer referral to genetic counseling/germline evaluation.
- Anesthesia/anticoagulation: patient on no medications and no blood thinners — no agents to hold; standard sedation, clear for scheduling.
- No known drug allergies.
- Provide bowel prep instructions; return precautions for any interval bleeding or symptoms.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Getting your first colonoscopy because colon cancer runs in your family (your mother and your brother were both diagnosed young)
• You have no symptoms yourself and have never had cancer — this test is to screen and stay ahead of it
• Because your relatives were diagnosed at young ages, we screen you earlier and more often than usual
• The option of seeing a genetic counselor to look into whether this pattern is inherited

Next steps
• We will schedule your screening colonoscopy and send you the bowel prep instructions.
• You take no medications and no blood thinners, so there is nothing to stop before the procedure.
• We are offering a referral to genetic counseling for you to consider.
• Call us if you notice any rectal bleeding, blood in your stool, or changes in your bowel habits before the procedure.
